speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two. OK, tell us about '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing, I can't understand how yesterday's friends could be today's enemies. When, when they came in, the Germans, and they were taking us to all kind of work. And then the-- then came the holidays. On, on all those kinds of works, we were taken-- they take us to paint houses. I was never on a ladder until this-- on a step ladder-- until they took me to paint a building, a house, and all the other people-- old people, and [? this. ?] Until we were continued, so working, living at home, until the Yom Kippur [? war ?] came-- Yom Kippur came, and we were all in shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Of Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we hear that [? there were-- ?] there was an aksia in Podhajce.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they were taking people to kill. An aksia is when they take people and they just kill them. They tell them they are, they're going to take them somewhere else, and they take and kill them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at this time we saw-- the gentiles came over and said that they were coming. They-- in Podhajce is an aksia. So we all fled from the schul to the fields to hide, because in our, in our city, in Zawalow, was a small congregation, but from around the villages they used to come, and it was a lot of people then in the shul. And we went in hiding via-- until the next day of Yom Kippur.

speaker: interviewee
text: After Yom Kippur, I recall that we came home from the hiding to our houses in the late in the afternoon. And we continued the life normally until we hear that we will have-- they are bringing from all the villages to our city, all the Jews. They concentrated them from one place to the other place, and we were-- they brought them to our place. In our house there were a few families living, and by some other people, because there wasn't place where to a few, a few families in one house. And at this time they took out-- in Podhajce, we hear the next day, because we were still somebody was there in, in contact with Podhajce. And they took-- they killed 1,300 people, which I have a monument which I'll show you later on. And this Yom Kippur, they came into our shul in Podhajce and they took out the rabbi and the other [? prayers, ?] and placed where there was garbage-- a garbage hall. They took them out in their prayer shawls and they killed them there. And also in this place, it was called old city, they may-- they killed 1,300 people in this day. The next day they were talking that the earth was like, like moving. Because they, because there were a lot of people who still lived there, and they were put into the grave alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You heard about this? How--

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard about this, yeah, because we had relatives there in Podhajce, and we were still in our city. They brought to our city those other from the other one. And six weeks later was another aksia.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Zawalow now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Zawalow. Six weeks later was the other aksia. No, by the other aksia they, it was in the old city, that they killed the 1,300 people. And this first--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, so six weeks later they did it in the old city, which was how far from Zawalow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Yom Kippur they did this thing. Six weeks later, they brought all from Zawalow, all the people, to Podhajce, and they made the ghetto in Podhajce. The ghetto in Podhajce-- no, they brought us all in to the, to Podhajce. And there was another-- when we came to Podhajce we were, we had-- the young people used to go to work in our city-- and I missed out this before-- was a [INAUDIBLE]. It's like a farm owner. And we had to pay them that he shall take us to work. But we were safer than to be in Podhajce, because they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just sit back, you're fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were safer to be in there. So my, my parents tried the [INAUDIBLE], and other parents, the young people, they went to Zawalow. We, we were there working. And then when we hear the six week, that there is another aksia, we were coming every week home. We worked a whole week, and one day we came home to the, to Podhajce. But we had to pay them that we shall work by them. When we were in, when we hear that there is another aksia, so we didn't came in this day, but later, the second day after the aksia. And at this time my father was taken-- this was the other aksia-- my father was taken to Belzec. They were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Belzec?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. But this was a place where they-- a camp-- and they used to kill all the people. And this other aksia, we-- I wasn't home. We, we had the bunker. My, my father went in hiding, and my uncle went in hiding, and other people there. My mother refused to go into hiding. And they discovered the bunker and they took them out. They brought them to-- they took them to Belzec and put [? them ?] [? there. ?] My mother survived. She walked out just in, behind a bush in the garden there, and she survived. I had a cousin then. She was killed in there in bed, because they couldn't, she couldn't go out. So they killed her in bed. And they, they brought hundreds and hundreds, thousands of people to, to the trains. And they put them in, in wagons for cattle-- for animals. They took them to Belzec. This was when my father was taken away. I'm so mixed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. Was your brother taken with your father also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My brother was taken this when we were at home. I didn't say this. They were taking men to-- when we were still in Zawalow-- we can not go back to this tape, could we?

speaker: interviewer
text: We can go back. You can tell me about your brother. Tell me. It's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were still in Zawalow--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell me what year.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was in 1940 or '41. No, '41. It was in 1941 still. But in the beginning, so they took him to a camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: They took--

speaker: interviewee
text: To a work camp--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to Buchenwald, and to Plaszow. And I went from there, from home, to bring him some food when I came there, where he was in the camp. And he was only 14 years old. He was older and so tired he couldn't even walk no more. He didn't want to, he didn't want to come. Always when, when they were working there was music playing, such loud music, music, and they were beaten. When I, when I came there and he was told that his sister came. I came with another girl. Her husband was taken also to this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took, they told him, and he walked out of-- he got up when he heard that I am there. He said I, to me, I don't know where I took the strength to get up and to come to see you. And I brought him some food. He was so hungry that he hardly can, could talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said-- I was trying, maybe there is some place that we can free him, but there was nothing that we could do. And when I came back home there were such collaborators that they used to take some ransom money, and sometimes they freed somebody. When I him them that we, we will try everything when I came home. So we paid some money to take him out, to get him out, and it, it was too late. He was killed already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who you paid the money to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. My father did it in, at home. So he was killed when we were still home. [? And I-- ?] but I don't want to go back to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shall I go--I mean, can you--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're doing fine, so now--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, because this belongs to the other tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK, that's fine. So tell me now about your father. Let's go back forward now. So tell me about your father. You were talking about that before. Your father was in the bunker. Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they discovered it, and they took them to Belzec, to the, to the station. A lot of people full of-- hundreds, and hundreds, and thousands of people they are loaded into the, to the wagons. And they brought them to Belzec, and he was killed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how soon afterwards this happened, that he was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. They were travelling-- it's a little far travel. There's a lot of people died in the wagons. Some escaped, so later we heard this. Who knows how?

speaker: interviewer
text: So who told you that your father was killed? When did you find this out?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew that they were all killed. We never heard. We never saw him again. When we came the, the next day, when he was still at the station there, and he asked to a policeman, watch out, because the children have to come now. They shouldn't-- [? when ?] we were hired to work-- they shouldn't come in now. And they shouldn't go into this, that we shall be taken away down there, too. Even at this time, he remembered his children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, do you want to tell me?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can we look and talk?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after this aksia, they build the ghetto. The ghetto was--

speaker: interviewer
text: So now it was just you and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The life in the ghetto was-- it was boarded all around with boards and wires, and I was on the end with my mother when we, when we had to come from there from our city. So they gave us a room in the ghetto where five families were living in one small room. Everybody had a bed, and this was where we had to live. I lived then with my mother. There was another family, which, which I recall as only a sister and a brother. And they had three children-- three small children, like, three, five, and seven. And they put-- there was a box. The two children were sleeping in a box because at this time, when we were in the ghetto, an epidemic started-- the typhus. And his brother was sick with typhus, so she separated the children. They slept two in a box and one on top of the box. It has a door, so she put a pull, a pull, that they shall have air-- like slender. There was another child, and she was sitting every night and watching the brother and those three children of hers. On every, in every bed was some, another family. And the life in ghetto was so terrible. We were at night, every time, building some different bunkers. And we were on posts watching if an aksia doesn't start again. We were hungry. We didn't have food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with any of your relatives, or just your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with, just with my mother. There was another cousin. Another cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was the ghetto? Do you know about how many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were thousands of people from all around, yeah. It was, like, a few blocks. Let's say about three blocks surrounded with board and this. We-- I recall we used to have one day of marketing that we could go out and buy some food. They used to bring the, the farmers, and we used to change, like, jewelry or clothing-- whatever we had. We used to change for a few potatoes, a little flour, some kind of piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was just one day a week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we were beaten coming home, coming back, coming-- going there on the way. And every day in the ghetto you hear this one-- whenever they felt, they came in in the ghetto and killed a few people, whoever was outside. And we used to go out to work, and they took us all kind of work from the ghetto, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do? You worked outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- I worked for a while at this farm where, where we were paid-- that we paid them to hire us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came in every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, once a week. But after the second aksia when the ghetto was built, they stopped taking us there. They didn't let us in no more to work, even if we paid them. So they didn't let us work there no more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do all day in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Crying. Looking for the-- we used to go out to work, too, and, we were beaten there. They had, like, clothes, they separate-- they gave us to separate clothes, to clean, to clean their houses, to wash clothes, such things. Every humiliating thing they could give us, they did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother did that also, or just you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just I.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother stayed in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed in the ghetto. She wasn't taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did they liquidate? Did they liquidate the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: They liquidated the ghetto in '43, '41-- '43, after Shabbat I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in May of '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So do you remember the day that they liquidated the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember. They-- there was another aksia. And-- oh, my mother died of typhus then. And I was sick in the ghetto of typhus, but I survived. And then, when they liquidate the ghetto, I-- we went in hiding. I was living with a cousin at this time. And I went to another cousin to tell him that, because I heard it first, that there was an aksia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody came in and told us that there is an aksia, because there was always people out watching, like on posts. We never slept, everybody, but somebody was always looking out. So we went, one ran to tell the others, and we wanted to go into hiding. My, my husband's brother-in-law, they had a mill there and we, they made a bunker there. We wanted to go into this bunker, but it was too late and we couldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went into the-- it was near the Judenrat. We went in there. My cousin wanted to get a badge. When he gets a badge as a policeman-- you pay, he had to pay for it-- they would, so he'll survive. And I went to tell him, but they-- he couldn't get it. So that aksia started, and we walked out, and this was it. He told me, you go this way and I go this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt very bad, but I went. And there was, nearby, a garage with old furniture thrown around, and went in there behind a sink. And there was only place that I could stay on my knees. And I was there a whole day and a whole night. The aksia was going on. You were hearing constant shooting, and people crying and yelling that they, they were catched, to go. They discovered them from bunkers. They have to-- they took them and killed them. Some there, and some they in groups they took. And there was a whole day-- there was like two doors in this garage. People were coming in and out, and they were taking them to, to be killed. I can't talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this garage outside of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the ghetto. In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you there with other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was alone. Just with a cousin, a girl, another girl, behind this closet. A whole day was going on the aksia. They were taking people, and bringing them, and killing them in the ghetto, whatever they can. The next, the next day, they took--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to start on--

speaker: interviewee
text: They said they're going to liquidate the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll start there on the next tape, OK?
